event: Early human presence in the region is evidenced by Middle Paleolithic stone tools found near the Pripyat and Dnieper river basins.
Upper Paleolithic settlements at Yurovichi and Berdyzh become some of the oldest known human campsites in present-day Belarus.
Neolithic hunter-gatherer cultures spread across the forests and river valleys, leaving pottery and flint-mining sites.
Bronze Age Corded Ware and related cultures introduce metalworking and animal husbandry to the region.
Iron Age Milograd and Dnieper-Dvina cultures establish fortified hillfort settlements across the territory.
Early East Slavic tribes including the Krivichs, Dregoviches, and Radimichs settle the lands of modern Belarus.
Polotsk is first mentioned in the Primary Chronicle, marking the emergence of one of the earliest East Slavic principalities.
Christianity begins spreading through the lands of Polotsk in the wake of the Christianization of Kievan Rus'.
The Principality of Polotsk fights the Battle of the Nemiga River against Kievan princes, an event recorded in 'The Tale of Igor's Campaign'.
Kievan Grand Prince Mstislav exiles the rebellious princes of Polotsk to Byzantium, temporarily subjugating the principality.
Saint Euphrosyne of Polotsk commissions the famed jeweled Cross of Euphrosyne, a masterpiece of medieval craftsmanship.
Belarusian lands largely escape direct Mongol conquest, allowing local principalities to retain greater autonomy than other Rus' lands.
Mindaugas is crowned, and the Grand Duchy of Lithuania begins incorporating Belarusian principalities including Navahrudak.
Grand Duke Gediminas consolidates Lithuanian rule over most Belarusian lands, with Old Belarusian becoming the chancery language.
The Union of Krewo binds the Grand Duchy of Lithuania to the Kingdom of Poland through a dynastic marriage.
Forces from Belarusian lands fight in the Battle of Grunwald, decisively defeating the Teutonic Knights.
Francysk Skaryna prints the first Bible in a Belarusian-influenced Ruthenian language, pioneering East Slavic book printing.
The first Statute of Lithuania, written in Old Belarusian, codifies one of Europe's most advanced legal systems.
The Union of Lublin merges Lithuania and Poland into the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, incorporating Belarusian lands.
The Union of Brest creates the Greek Catholic (Uniate) Church, which becomes dominant among Belarusian peasants.
The Russo-Polish War devastates Belarusian lands, killing roughly half the population over the following years.
Polish replaces Old Belarusian as the official chancery language of the Grand Duchy, accelerating cultural polonization.
The First Partition of Poland transfers eastern Belarusian territory to the Russian Empire.
The Second Partition of Poland brings central Belarus, including Minsk, under Russian imperial control.
The Third Partition completes the absorption of all Belarusian lands into the Russian Empire.
Napoleon's Grande Armée marches through Belarus, and the disastrous crossing of the Berezina River seals its retreat.
The Russian authorities abolish the Uniate Church, forcibly converting Belarusian Greek Catholics to Orthodoxy.
Kastus Kalinowski leads an uprising against Russian rule and publishes the first Belarusian-language newspaper, 'Muzhytskaya Prauda'.
The newspaper 'Nasha Niva' begins publication, becoming a cornerstone of the modern Belarusian national revival.
The Belarusian People's Republic is proclaimed in Minsk amid the upheaval of World War I and the Russian Revolution.
The Byelorussian Soviet Socialist Republic is established under Bolshevik control.
The Treaty of Riga partitions Belarus, awarding western lands to Poland and leaving the east in Soviet hands.
The Byelorussian SSR becomes a founding republic of the Soviet Union.
Stalin's Great Purge devastates the Belarusian cultural and political elite, with mass executions at Kurapaty near Minsk.
Western Belarus is annexed from Poland into the Byelorussian SSR following the Soviet invasion of Poland.
Nazi Germany invades, occupying all of Belarus and beginning a brutal regime that destroys hundreds of villages.
The village of Khatyn is burned with its inhabitants, becoming a symbol of the massacres that killed roughly a quarter of the population.
Operation Bagration liberates Belarus from Nazi occupation, ending one of the deadliest chapters in its history.
The Byelorussian SSR becomes a founding member of the United Nations, holding a seat separate from the USSR.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster contaminates large areas of southern Belarus, with lasting health and environmental consequences.
The Byelorussian SSR declares state sovereignty as the Soviet Union begins to disintegrate.
Belarus declares full independence and helps found the Commonwealth of Independent States at the Belovezha Accords.
Alexander Lukashenko wins the first presidential election, beginning a long authoritarian rule.
A referendum restores Soviet-era state symbols and makes Russian a co-official language alongside Belarusian.
Belarus and Russia sign a treaty creating the Union State, deepening political and economic integration.
A controversial referendum removes presidential term limits, allowing Lukashenko to seek indefinite re-election.
Mass protests erupt after a disputed presidential election, met with a sweeping government crackdown.
Belarus serves as a staging ground for Russia's invasion of Ukraine, drawing expanded Western sanctions.
